_comment: Sankat-Mochan LoRa gateway. Copy to config.json and edit. Every value here can be overridden by an env var of the same dotted path, upper-cased with '.' -> '__' and prefixed SANKAT_ (e.g. SANKAT_LORA__TX_POWER_DBM=10).

run._comment: Which radio node(s) THIS board runs. A single Pi holding both radios lists both (the original bench setup). Once radio A moves to the UNO Q, that board lists only 'field' and the Pi lists only 'gateway' — same code, two boxes, still bridged only by 433 MHz. Each name here must have a matching block under 'radios'. The RF startup probe (which needs both radios on one board) auto-disables when only one node runs.

lora._voice_tx_repeats_comment: How many times each VOICE chunk is sent on the air (text/SOS use tx_repeats). A voice clip is all-or-nothing — every one of its ~16-45 chunks must arrive or it is unplayable — so a single shot per chunk loses whole clips on a lossy hop. 3 gets most chunks across on the first pass; the NACK repair loop recovers the rest. Raise to 4-5 on a poor RF link (costs proportionally more airtime); SOS is unaffected (exempt critical lane + mid-clip yield).

field._comment: Radio A. Each radio has a 'transport': "spi" (wired to THIS board's SPI, the original single-Pi bench), "serial" (a separate Arduino LoRa modem on USB, /dev/ttyACM*), or "bridge" (the Arduino UNO Q's OWN on-board MCU, reached over the Router Bridge socket — the single-board field node, see arduino-unoq/). On the UNO Q field board set transport="bridge" in config.json (no serial_port needed). cs/rst_gpio/dio0_gpio are used only by the "spi" transport and ignored for serial/bridge; they stay here so the old two-radio Pi bench still runs unchanged.

gateway._comment: Radio B — stays at the relief camp with the Pi, wired to its SPI. Verified wiring.

ble._peers_comment: There is no peer list any more. Phones are matched by the role they picked in the app, which they broadcast in their advertisement: every RESPONDER attaches to the gateway radio, everyone else to the field radio. Pinning by MAC would not work anyway — Android rotates its Bluetooth address between runs.

uplink._comment: POST each envelope the gateway node accepts to the AI-PC dashboard. Never put secrets here; use env vars. outbox_max caps the durable SOS queue: on overflow we REFUSE new low-urgency envelopes and raise an alarm, but NEVER drop an already-accepted one (see mesh-transmission.md D4).

voice._comment: Pi-side of the voice NACK repair loop. When gateway reassembly stalls, we ask the victim phone to resend the pieces the LoRa hop dropped. node_id is this gateway's 4-char requester id on the mesh (alnum, <=4 chars; keep it distinct from any phone id). nack_quiet_s is how long a clip must be silent before we ask again (a touch longer than the phone's 3s so phone-to-phone repair goes first). upload_concurrency is BOTH the voice HTTP thread-pool size AND the per-pass upload semaphore (signed-off cross-owner contract: default pool stays radio-only, voice gets its own bounded pool of this many workers). upload_connect_s/upload_read_s bound each POST so a dead AI-PC can't pin a worker.

ingest._comment: All inbound mesh frames (BLE + LoRa) are untrusted (CLAUDE.md #8). Each MeshNode drains a bounded queue; frames over queue_max are dropped-and-logged rather than spawning unbounded tasks. global_frames_per_s is a spoof-proof ceiling on TOTAL accepted frames/sec (origins are unauthenticated, so a per-origin cap is bypassable — the global cap is the real DoS defence, see mesh-transmission.md D1/M4). seen_max bounds the dedup ring.
